topic.what: Systematic mapping of traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) formulas used for H. pylori-associated gastritis onto canonical intracellular signaling pathways — NF-kB, MAPK, and Wnt/beta-catenin.
topic.how: Literature survey aggregating mechanistic pharmacology studies; each formula's active constituents are linked to pathway nodes (e.g., IKK inhibition for NF-kB, ERK/JNK modulation for MAPK, Frizzled/GSK-3beta for Wnt).
topic.why_matters: Provides a molecular-level explanatory bridge between empirical TCM prescriptions and Western pathway pharmacology, enabling rational drug target identification and synergy prediction.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Number of canonical signaling pathways mechanistically annotated for TCM anti-gastritis formulas
characteristics.value: Three major pathways covered (NF-kB, MAPK, Wnt/beta-catenin)
characteristics.vs_baseline: Prior reviews typically addressed a single pathway
characteristics.evidence: Survey synthesis across indexed mechanistic studies
characteristics.context: Coverage limited to studies with explicit pathway reporter or knockout evidence

characteristics.what_it_measures: Degree to which individual TCM formulas are attributed to distinct vs. overlapping pathway targets
characteristics.value: Substantial cross-pathway overlap; most formulas modulate all three pathways to varying degrees
characteristics.evidence: Cross-tabulation of formula ingredients vs. pathway nodes
characteristics.context: Multi-target nature expected given polypharmacy structure of TCM

good_for: Researchers seeking molecular entry points for TCM-derived anti-H. pylori compounds
Drug repurposing pipelines targeting NF-kB or Wnt in gastric inflammation
Network pharmacology studies requiring pathway annotation of TCM herbs
not_for: Clinical decision-making without further in-vivo validation
requires: Access to primary mechanistic studies cited

novelty: Simultaneous multi-pathway mapping across a broad set of TCM formulas — not limited to single-herb or single-pathway analysis
Explicit mechanistic bridge between ethnopharmacological practice and Western pathway ontology
open_problems: Causal directionality between pathway modulation and clinical symptom resolution remains unclear
In-vivo pharmacokinetics of key TCM actives at pathway-relevant concentrations not established
Wnt/beta-catenin branch particularly under-studied relative to NF-kB
